name: Discovery & Requirements
goal: Understand before building: validate assumptions, produce an approved plan, and get a green light before Phase 1. No production code.
definition_of_done: Core requirements doc approved by Neil (docs/requirements.md)
Agent boundary analysis approved by Neil (docs/agent-boundaries.md)
Security and auth requirements approved by Neil (docs/security-requirements.md)
Data and state architecture approved by Neil (docs/data-architecture.md)
CLI and self-building requirements approved by Neil (docs/cli-requirements.md)
MCP SDK + two-layer pattern spike: GO/NO-GO verdict + ADR written
kagent evaluation spike: GO/NO-GO verdict + ADR written
GitOps operator + Crossplane on k3s spike: Flux vs Argo decision + ADR written
Local model evaluation spike: task performance measurements + model assignments documented
Validation loop proof of concept spike: standalone PoC runs + ADR written
Provisioning agent boundary design approved by Neil (docs/provisioning-agent.md)
Project template design approved by Neil (docs/project-template.md)
High-level plan phases 1-3 approved by Neil (docs/plan.md)
constraints: No production code — spikes and proofs of concept only
No Lume-specific implementation — evaluate tools and patterns only
Everything disposable — nothing from Phase 0 ships in Phase 1
Write conclusions, not just notes — every spike produces a decision
am_i_done.py must remain deterministic — no inference in the gate
Existing pytest suite must pass throughout
learning_goals: Can the two-layer MCP pattern work in practice (orchestrator MCP calling agent MCP)?
Is kagent a viable agent runtime for Lume, or do we build our own?
Which GitOps operator (Flux vs Argo) fits the Lume architecture better?
Which local models can handle which roles at acceptable quality and speed?
Can a validation loop be built that is truly deterministic (no LLM inference for pass/fail)?
assumptions_to_validate: The two-layer MCP pattern scales: orchestrator can coordinate 5+ agents via internal MCP without latency becoming a problem
kagent provides enough of the agent runtime that we don't need to build our own k8s scheduling
Local models (qwen2.5-coder:7b) are good enough for routine code tasks without cloud escalation on every call
A single devcontainer with k3s + all dependencies is practical on a developer laptop (8-16GB RAM)
Declarative GitOps config separation is enforceable — Lume can be prevented from applying infra directly
next_phase_trigger: All DoD items verified and Neil gives green light. Run /phase-complete.
